title: Inpatient Hospice Admission for End-Stage Metastatic Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma with Refractory Pain, Nausea, Terminal Delirium, and Terminal Secretions: ELNEC-Based Comfort Care, Caregiver Support, and Last Hours of Life
subtitle: HOSPICE ELIGIBILITY: Life Expectancy ≤6 mo If Disease Runs Natural Course (Medicare Hospice Benefit); SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT Tailored to MECHANISM (Opioid LADDER + ROTATION + Breakthrough; Mechanism-Based ANTIEMETICS — Vestibular/Mechanical/CTZ/ICP; HALOPERIDOL for Terminal DELIRIUM + Remove Contributors; GLYCOPYRROLATE/ATROPINE for TERMINAL SECRETIONS + Repositioning + Family Education; Low-Dose MORPHINE for DYSPNEA; LORAZEPAM for Anxiety; BOWEL REGIMEN with ALL Opioids); CAREGIVER FATIGUE → RESPITE + Bereavement; CULTURAL + SPIRITUAL + Family-Centered Communication; LAST HOURS Education (Mottling + Cheyne-Stokes + Apnea + Declining Responsiveness — Family Knows What to Expect); RN SCOPE at Death (Pronouncement Varies by State); 13-Month BEREAVEMENT Per Medicare Hospice Benefit

patient.name: S. Whitfield
patient.code: DNR/DNI documented + signed POLST in chart (Allow Natural Death); comfort-focused care; family present + aligned with goals of care
patient.admitDate: Admitted to inpatient hospice unit at 1400 today; transitioned from 3 months of home hospice care due to refractory pain + nausea + new terminal delirium + caregiver fatigue (wife age 60 + adult daughter providing 24/7 care; daughter took FMLA from work)
patient.room: Inpatient Hospice Unit Bed 4 (private room with family bed + recliner + quiet environment + spiritual care + bedside courtyard window); family present at admission (wife, adult daughter, adult son flying in tonight)

vitals.time: Last home hospice visit yesterday afternoon
vitals.temp: 37.6°C; weight 48 kg (down from 78 kg pre-diagnosis 8 months ago = cachexia; baseline BMI 26 → current BMI 16); jaundiced sclera + skin; abdominal distension; bilateral ankle edema

vitals.spo2: 91% on RA (declined nasal cannula — gentle fan + repositioning instead per his prior wishes; SpO2 monitoring de-emphasized per comfort-focused care)
vitals.temp: 38.0°C (terminal fever — common; treat for comfort with acetaminophen if patient appears uncomfortable; cooling measures gently); mottling beginning at knees + lower legs
38.2°C; mottling extending up thighs; minimally responsive to voice (responds to deep touch with grimace); family at bedside; pain managed; secretions managed

labs.v: HOSPICE ELIGIBILITY (Medicare Hospice Benefit): life expectancy ≤6 months if disease runs natural course (certified by hospice medical director + attending); patient enrolled 3 months ago after declining 2nd-line chemotherapy (failed first-line FOLFIRINOX with disease progression at 6 months; offered gem-nab-pac 2nd line; declined further chemotherapy after discussion of toxicity vs minimal expected benefit + preference for quality time); GOALS OF CARE clarified through serial family meetings: COMFORT-FOCUSED CARE (Allow Natural Death — AND), no resuscitation/intubation/ICU transfer, no artificial nutrition/hydration unless explicitly for symptom relief (not life-extension), no antibiotics unless for comfort (e.g., UTI causing discomfort), focus on PAIN + SYMPTOM RELIEF + DIGNITY + FAMILY PRESENCE + SPIRITUAL/EMOTIONAL SUPPORT; signed POLST in chart with full DNR/DNI + Allow Natural Death; HEALTHCARE PROXY (wife) activated; advance directives reviewed at every transition; PROGNOSIS at admission to inpatient hospice: hours to days based on functional decline + vital trajectory + symptom burden + multi-organ involvement
labs.r: MEDICARE HOSPICE BENEFIT eligibility: certified life expectancy ≤6 mo if disease runs natural course; recertification at 90-90-60-60-day intervals + every 60 days indefinitely if eligibility persists. SERVICES INCLUDED: nursing + aide + social work + chaplain + bereavement + medical director + medications + equipment + supplies + 24/7 access + respite (5 days inpatient q month) + inpatient (general inpatient for acute symptom management OR continuous home care for crisis) + routine home care. GOALS OF CARE conversations are ONGOING + evolve as patient + family + disease progress. POLST translates wishes into medical orders that follow the patient across settings

labs.n: DIAGNOSIS: metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (head of pancreas, locally advanced, multiple liver mets + peritoneal carcinomatosis)
labs.v: 62M with metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PDAC) diagnosed 8 months ago after weight loss + jaundice + abdominal pain → CT showed pancreatic head mass with liver metastases + peritoneal carcinomatosis + CA 19-9 markedly elevated; EUS-FNA biopsy confirmed adenocarcinoma; staged IV; ECOG 4 (in bed, fully dependent — was ECOG 1 at diagnosis); progressed through FOLFIRINOX (4 cycles, disease progression); declined 2nd-line; enrolled in home hospice 3 months ago; biliary obstruction managed with biliary stent 5 months ago; gastric outlet obstruction not present (no NG/PEG; able to take small sips/bites by mouth recently — now declining); WEIGHT LOSS 30 kg over 8 months (~38% of body weight — cancer cachexia); JAUNDICE (T bili likely >15 — not currently labs-driven in hospice care); ABDOMINAL DISTENSION from ascites + organomegaly (paracentesis offered for comfort if symptomatic); BILATERAL ANKLE EDEMA (hypoalbuminemia + venous return + cachexia)
labs.r: PANCREATIC ADENOCARCINOMA (PDAC) is one of the most aggressive solid tumors. Median overall survival for metastatic disease: 6-12 months with chemotherapy; longer in select subgroups (BRCA-mutated → PARP inhibitors); shorter without treatment. CACHEXIA (involuntary weight loss + muscle wasting + anorexia) is multifactorial (tumor cytokines + reduced intake + malabsorption + metabolic derangement); not reversible with nutrition alone. Patient on standard end-of-life trajectory. Symptoms throughout course: pain (visceral + neuropathic from celiac plexus/retroperitoneal invasion), nausea (GI obstruction, gastroparesis, opioid-related, ICP from brain mets if present), jaundice + pruritus, ascites, fatigue, weight loss + cachexia, anxiety + depression + spiritual/existential distress

labs.n: PAIN MANAGEMENT — refractory pain requiring inpatient escalation
labs.v: PAIN at admission: 7/10 visceral abdominal pain (upper abdomen + back — classic pancreatic + retroperitoneal/celiac plexus invasion); NEUROPATHIC component (burning, shooting, allodynia in epigastric region); SOMATIC + ISCHEMIC bone pain from rib mets (recently identified by clinical exam); HOME REGIMEN: morphine extended-release 60 mg PO q8h + morphine immediate-release 15 mg PO/SL q1h PRN breakthrough (limited tolerance with declining PO intake + nausea); subcutaneous PCA initiated 1 week ago by hospice (morphine basal 4 mg/hr + 2 mg q15 min PRN breakthrough) — pain remained 6-8/10 with frequent breakthrough use; ADJUVANTS: gabapentin 300 mg PO TID for neuropathic (held due to nausea + reduced PO); methadone considered but not started; OPIOID-INDUCED CONSTIPATION addressed with senna + polyethylene glycol + methylnaltrexone PRN (no bowel movement x 4 days at admission — concern); INPATIENT ESCALATION: switched to subcutaneous hydromorphone PCA (rotated from morphine for renal-induced metabolite accumulation + improved efficacy; conversion ~5:1 morphine:hydromorphone subcutaneous so 4 mg/hr morphine ~= 0.8 mg/hr hydromorphone — adjusted for incomplete cross-tolerance starting at 0.5 mg/hr basal + 0.2 mg q15 min PRN); KETAMINE infusion 0.1 mg/kg/hr low-dose subcutaneous adjuvant for refractory pain + opioid-sparing (avoiding sedation/dysphoria with low-dose start); INTRATHECAL pump considered but not feasible given prognosis hours-days; CELIAC PLEXUS BLOCK considered but not now (would require procedure + prognosis too short); ROUND-THE-CLOCK acetaminophen 650 mg PR q6h (around-the-clock for fever + opioid-sparing); DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg IV/SQ daily continued (multi-purpose: bone mets pain + anorexia + nausea + fatigue + general well-being in cancer)
labs.r: PAIN MANAGEMENT in end-stage cancer follows WHO ANALGESIC LADDER + individualized approach. Most cancer pain controllable with appropriate regimen. PRINCIPLES: regular round-the-clock dosing + as-needed breakthrough; mechanism-based selection (opioids + adjuvants for nociceptive; gabapentin/pregabalin/duloxetine/TCAs for neuropathic; corticosteroids for bone/inflammation; bisphosphonates/radiation for bone mets); OPIOID ROTATION when one opioid not effective (cross-tolerance is incomplete — switch + reduce dose 25-50% then titrate); SUBCUTANEOUS route when oral not feasible (rapid uptake; reliable absorption; less burdensome than IV); PCA empowers patient + reduces delay; KETAMINE low-dose adjuvant for refractory pain; ALL OPIOIDS need a BOWEL REGIMEN (senna + osmotic + methylnaltrexone for opioid-induced refractory); intrathecal pump + celiac block + radiation for selected patients with longer prognosis; ACETAMINOPHEN round-the-clock + dexamethasone often added for multipurpose benefit. UNDERTREATING PAIN is a common error — be aggressive within comfort goals

labs.n: NAUSEA MANAGEMENT — mechanism-based (multi-mechanism nausea in this patient)
labs.v: NAUSEA at admission moderate (3-5/10 by patient report when able; family + RN observation: grimacing + retching + dry heaving); MULTIPLE MECHANISMS contributing: (1) OPIOID-INDUCED (chemoreceptor trigger zone CTZ activation; first 1-2 weeks of new opioid, with dose escalation, or with rotation); (2) GASTROPARESIS / mechanical (pancreatic mass + ascites + delayed gastric emptying); (3) METABOLIC (hyperbilirubinemia, possible uremia, hypercalcemia from bone mets — not labs-driven in hospice); (4) ANTICIPATORY/anxiety component; (5) INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE excluded (no brain mets known); MECHANISM-BASED ANTIEMETICS: HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg PO/SQ q6h scheduled + q4h PRN (CTZ blockade — best for opioid + metabolic nausea); METOCLOPRAMIDE 10 mg PO/SQ q6h (PROKINETIC — best for gastroparesis/mechanical; caution: contraindicated in complete bowel obstruction + EPS/QT risk); ONDANSETRON 4-8 mg PO/SQ q8h PRN (5HT3 — useful for chemo/radiation; less for end-of-life nausea but used for refractory); OLANZAPINE 2.5-5 mg PO/SL daily (broad-spectrum antiemetic + appetite stimulation + mood + sleep — particularly useful in end-of-life multi-mechanism nausea); DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg daily (continued for cerebral + ICP + multi-purpose); LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4h PRN (anticipatory + anxiety + sedation); GINGER + acupressure + small frequent meals (when tolerated) + cool foods + low-stimulus environment; AVOID PROCHLORPERAZINE (more sedating + EPS risk in elderly); avoid PROMETHAZINE in elderly (sedation + delirium + EPS)
labs.r: NAUSEA MANAGEMENT at end of life follows MECHANISM-BASED approach (Brooks/Twycross). VESTIBULAR nausea (motion-related) → meclizine/cyclizine/scopolamine. MECHANICAL/gastric stasis nausea → metoclopramide (prokinetic; avoid if complete obstruction) or domperidone. CHEMORECEPTOR TRIGGER ZONE (opioid + metabolic) → haloperidol (gold standard) or olanzapine (broad-spectrum). INCREASED ICP → dexamethasone + 5HT3 antagonist. ANTICIPATORY/anxiety → lorazepam. Multi-mechanism nausea (this patient) → combination therapy. AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly (sedation/EPS/delirium). Reassess + titrate frequently. Address contributing factors (constipation, dehydration in some cases, anxiety, food preferences). Olanzapine + dexamethasone + haloperidol combination often effective

labs.v: DELIRIUM features developing past 48 hr: acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganized thinking, altered consciousness; hyperactive components (restlessness, picking at sheets, agitation, calling out for deceased mother) + hypoactive components (somnolence, withdrawal); CONTRIBUTING FACTORS: terminal disease progression (cerebral hypoperfusion + metabolic derangement), opioid metabolite accumulation in renal decline (rotated from morphine to hydromorphone for this), hypercalcemia from bone mets (no labs in hospice — empiric consideration), hypoxia, dehydration, urinary retention, fecal impaction, hyperbilirubinemia, ICU/hospital environment (single quiet room helps), benzodiazepines (paradoxical agitation in elderly — minimize but lorazepam useful for anxiety + sedation in dying), opioids (titration but appropriate analgesia improves delirium); MANAGEMENT: REMOVE contributors when possible (opioid rotation done, ensure bowel emptying, urinary catheter for retention, gentle reorientation, family at bedside, quiet familiar environment, soft lighting, no overhead lights at night, minimize line/tube tethering, music/familiar voices, spiritual rituals); HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg PO/SQ q6h scheduled + q2h PRN for agitation (start low + titrate; preferred first-line for terminal delirium per palliative guidelines; less anticholinergic than typical antipsychotics; minimal sedation at low doses); CHLORPROMAZINE 12.5-25 mg PO/PR/SQ q4-6h PRN if more sedation needed; RISPERIDONE 0.5 mg PO BID or QUETIAPINE 25-50 mg PO/SL BID alternative; LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4-6h PRN for anxiety/agitation (use cautiously — paradoxical effect possible); AVOID anticholinergics (worsen delirium); FAMILY EDUCATION that terminal delirium is part of dying process — not pain or suffering per se; reassurance + presence + familiar voice; family may try to engage rationally but acknowledge limited cognitive engagement; meaningful presence + touch + music more impactful than verbal redirection
labs.r: TERMINAL DELIRIUM is common (~80% of patients in last days of life) + frequently distressing for family. MULTIFACTORIAL etiology. MANAGEMENT principles: remove contributors when feasible, treat reversible causes (urinary retention, fecal impaction, hypercalcemia in some cases, oversedation), pharmacologic management with haloperidol (gold standard) + benzodiazepine for breakthrough agitation; family education + reassurance + presence are central; quiet familiar environment + soft lighting + minimal stimulation; avoid restraints (not appropriate in EOL); spiritual + cultural rituals supported. FAMILY often more distressed than patient — explain what is happening (the patient is not in pain; this is part of dying; they may seem to be 'seeing' or 'speaking with' people who are not present — this is a common end-of-life experience). REASSURANCE + presence

labs.v: AUDIBLE GURGLING/RATTLING secretions emerging this afternoon (4-6 hr after admission); caused by accumulation of saliva + bronchial secretions in oropharynx + upper airway as patient loses cough + swallow ability + becomes less responsive; FAMILY DISTRESS HIGH (sounds 'like he's drowning' or 'suffocating') — but patient generally NOT distressed because diminished consciousness; MANAGEMENT: REPOSITION to side-lying or semi-prone with HOB elevated (drainage); GENTLE oral suctioning ONLY if accessible secretions (avoid deep suctioning — distressing + minimally effective + causes more secretions); ANTICHOLINERGIC AGENTS to reduce secretion production (NOT to dry existing secretions): GLYCOPYRROLATE 0.2-0.4 mg SQ/IV q4-6h PRN (preferred — does not cross BBB so less central anticholinergic effects + delirium); ATROPINE 0.4 mg SQ/SL q4-6h (alternative — crosses BBB so more potential for delirium; sublingual 1% ophthalmic drops also used); SCOPOLAMINE 1.5 mg transdermal patch q72h (slower onset but sustained); HYOSCYAMINE 0.125 mg PO/SL q4h PRN; OPTIMIZE EARLY — anticholinergics work best at PREVENTING accumulation rather than drying existing; FAMILY EDUCATION is the central intervention — explain what is happening (secretions are pooling because he no longer has the strength to clear them; this sounds distressing but is NOT distressing TO HIM because he is not aware of it; we are doing what we can but cannot completely eliminate the sound; this is a normal part of the last hours); REDUCE IV fluids if applicable (excessive fluid can worsen secretions in dying patient); LISTEN to family + acknowledge their distress; provide explanation + reassurance + presence
labs.r: TERMINAL SECRETIONS occur in 35-90% of dying patients in last hours/days. MANAGEMENT pearls: (1) family distress is often greater than patient distress, (2) anticholinergic agents work best when started EARLY before significant accumulation, (3) deep suctioning is counterproductive (stimulates more secretions + distresses patient), (4) repositioning + gentle oral care + family education are central, (5) glycopyrrolate preferred over atropine for less CNS effects + delirium risk, (6) reduce IV fluids if contributing, (7) FAMILY EDUCATION about what is happening + why we cannot eliminate it completely + that patient is not aware — this is the most important nursing intervention

labs.n: DYSPNEA + RESPIRATORY support at end of life
labs.v: DYSPNEA at admission moderate (SpO2 91% on RA but patient declined nasal cannula per prior expressed wishes); Cheyne-Stokes breathing pattern emerging (irregular crescendo-decrescendo with apneic pauses — common in last hours, indicates brain stem changes); MANAGEMENT: LOW-DOSE MORPHINE 2-5 mg PO/SL/SQ q4h PRN (and as part of PCA continuous) — gold standard for dyspnea at end of life; mechanism: reduces ventilatory drive + reduces sensation of dyspnea + reduces anxiety + reduces preload (with cardiogenic dyspnea); NO RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION at appropriate doses in opioid-tolerant patient with appropriate titration; LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4-6h PRN for anxiety component of dyspnea (synergistic with opioid); FAN at low setting blowing across face (vagal stimulation reduces sensation of dyspnea — evidence-based + non-pharmacologic); POSITIONING (semi-Fowler or upright if tolerated); minimize physical exertion (commode at bedside if mobile; bedpan; assist with all ADLs); supplemental O2 only if HYPOXIC + perceived to help patient comfort (not target SpO2 — comfort-focused); BiPAP/CPAP/HFNC not indicated in comfort-focused care unless explicitly helping symptoms (which is rare in terminal decline); INTUBATION not indicated (DNI documented + counter to goals); ANXIOLYTICS + ENVIRONMENTAL adjustments + REASSURANCE; OPIOID overdose is RARE when titrated for symptom; double-effect principle: aggressive symptom control even if foreseeable but unintended hastening of death is ethically + legally permissible when intent is comfort
labs.r: DYSPNEA at end of life is common + distressing. LOW-DOSE OPIOID is the gold standard (morphine evidence-based for dyspnea; mechanism not fully understood but multi-modal). Opioid 'fear' (respiratory depression) is largely unfounded at appropriate doses in opioid-tolerant patients with appropriate titration. ETHICAL principle of DOUBLE EFFECT: aggressive symptom management with appropriate intent (comfort) is permissible even if it foreseeably but unintentionally hastens death; this is supported by ethics + law + religious traditions. NON-PHARMACOLOGIC adjuncts (fan, positioning, environment, presence) are evidence-based + complementary. COMFORT-FOCUSED care does not target SpO2 — only patient comfort. Cheyne-Stokes is common in last hours + does not require intervention beyond family education

labs.n: OTHER SYMPTOM management (constipation, anxiety, anorexia/cachexia, oral care, dry mouth, fatigue, skin care, pruritus)
labs.v: CONSTIPATION: senna 8.6 mg PO BID + polyethylene glycol 17 g PO daily (when PO tolerated); methylnaltrexone 8-12 mg SQ q48h PRN (opioid-induced refractory — peripheral mu antagonist; does not cross BBB so does not reverse analgesia); bisacodyl suppository PR for impaction; manual disimpaction by RN if needed (gentle, with adequate analgesia + anxiolysis); ALL OPIOIDS REQUIRE A BOWEL REGIMEN — this is a frequently missed opportunity; ANXIETY: lorazepam 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4-6h PRN; chaplain/spiritual care; music; touch; family presence; communication about fears; ANOREXIA/CACHEXIA: do NOT force feeding — explain to family that anorexia is normal at end of life + forced feeding causes distress + does not improve outcomes; small bites of favorite foods if patient enjoys + tolerates; ice chips for pleasure; family education that loss of interest in food is part of dying; megestrol acetate or dronabinol/dexamethasone considered earlier in course but not for terminal patient; ORAL CARE q2-4h with foam swabs + water/saline + lip balm + glycerin avoidance (dries mucosa); DRY MOUTH from dehydration + mouth breathing + anticholinergics — sip water if able + ice chips + oral moisturizers + lip care; FATIGUE: rest + family + meaningful activities while alert (memory book, music, video calls with distant family); SKIN CARE: pressure injury prevention with frequent repositioning q2h (or more often if uncomfortable; less if causing distress in last hours), pressure-redistribution surface, barrier creams for incontinence, careful skin assessment; PRURITUS (from cholestasis): cool environment, oatmeal baths, naltrexone (low dose) or sertraline (sometimes effective), antihistamines limited use, topical menthol; URINARY RETENTION: catheterization if uncomfortable (Foley appropriate for comfort + reducing urinary frequency at end of life)
labs.r: Multi-symptom management is the work of hospice. EACH symptom requires assessment + treatment + reassessment. Comfort takes precedence over textbook nursing protocols (e.g., pressure injury prevention via q2h turning may be reduced if turning causes distress in last hours — comfort wins). FORCED feeding + hydration cause harm in dying patients without benefit — family education is critical (loss of interest in food + drink is part of dying; the body is not 'starving' in the way an otherwise healthy person would). Oral care is high-yield comfort. Symptom assessment is observation-based when patient cannot self-report

labs.v: FAMILY structure: wife (60, retired teacher, primary caregiver x 3 months home hospice, exhausted, sleep-deprived, hasn't had a meal away from bedside in weeks, lost 6 kg herself), adult daughter (32, took FMLA from work x 6 weeks, secondary caregiver, sleeping in shifts with mother, grieving in advance), adult son (35, lives across country, flying in tonight — feels guilt at distance), extended family (sister, brother, grandchildren) intermittently present + supportive, faith community (Methodist church + pastor + congregation prayer chain + meal train); CAREGIVER FATIGUE indicators: exhaustion, sleep deprivation, decreased self-care, social isolation, anxiety + depression, physical symptoms (the wife reports back pain + headaches + GI distress), anticipatory grief, financial stress + worry, role confusion (transition from spouse to nurse); INPATIENT HOSPICE ADMISSION provides RESPITE + RELIEF for family while continuing patient care; SOCIAL WORK consult activated for: assessment, resource linkage (caregiver support groups, meal delivery, transportation, financial counseling, FMLA paperwork extension, advance directive review, funeral planning discussion when family ready), bereavement preparation; CHAPLAIN consult (Methodist pastor is family's primary; hospital chaplain available if requested); BEREAVEMENT services per MEDICARE HOSPICE BENEFIT: 13-month bereavement support for family after patient's death (phone calls, in-person visits if requested, support groups, written materials, anniversary outreach); RESPITE for family during inpatient hospice (encouraged to leave bedside, eat meals, sleep elsewhere — RN provides updates); FAMILY MEETINGS with team to review prognosis + symptom plan + last hours expectations + post-death plans; CULTURAL + spiritual rituals supported (family's faith tradition includes communion + prayer at bedside; chaplain coordinates)
labs.r: CAREGIVER BURDEN is a recognized health issue with increased morbidity + mortality for the caregiver. INPATIENT HOSPICE provides respite when symptoms become unmanageable at home or when family is exhausted. HOSPICE BENEFIT services include 5 days/month inpatient respite. FAMILY-CENTERED care extends throughout patient's life + into bereavement. 13-MONTH BEREAVEMENT support per Medicare hospice regs (the year of firsts + anniversary). Faith community + spiritual support + cultural rituals are central. Social work coordinates resources. Anticipatory grief is normal + can be supported

labs.n: LAST HOURS of life — what to expect + family education
labs.v: LAST HOURS physiologic changes to expect + educate family about: CARDIOVASCULAR — tachycardia then bradycardia, hypotension, MOTTLING (cyanotic/purple discoloration of skin starting at extremities — knees, ankles, fingers — and moving centrally; indicates poor perfusion; PAINLESS for patient), cool extremities, weak/thready/absent peripheral pulses, irregular pulse; RESPIRATORY — Cheyne-Stokes pattern (irregular crescendo-decrescendo with apneic pauses 10-30 sec — common in last hours, indicates brainstem changes, NOT distressing for patient), AGONAL respirations (gasping/jaw-thrusting breathing — reflexive, NOT conscious distress, common in dying), terminal secretions (described above), pauses extending longer + longer until final apnea; NEUROLOGIC — declining responsiveness, may have lucid moments, eyes may remain open + unfocused, may seem to 'see' deceased relatives or have visions (encourage family not to dismiss — meaningful for many), may stop swallowing → secretions pool; loss of consciousness — HEARING is typically the LAST sense to fade (encourage family to continue speaking + meaningful presence even when patient appears unresponsive); GENITOURINARY — decreased then absent urine output (kidneys shutting down + dehydration physiologic); bowel/bladder incontinence may occur (compassionate care); GI — no interest in food/drink (NORMAL — do not force); SKIN — cool + mottled; nail beds cyanotic; jaundice may deepen; FAMILY EDUCATION about what to expect REDUCES FEAR + helps family be present meaningfully; what to do at moment of death — there is no urgency; the body can remain undisturbed for hours; family can take time to be present, say goodbye, perform rituals, call other family; nurse will pronounce or call attending for pronouncement (RN scope at death varies by state — in California RNs can pronounce expected hospice deaths per hospice protocol; document time of death, family present, circumstances, body care, family wishes for body care + funeral arrangements + autopsy generally not needed for expected hospice death + organ/tissue donation if applicable + bereavement contact)
labs.r: EDUCATING FAMILY about what to expect in last hours is one of the most important interventions of end-of-life care — reduces fear + supports meaningful presence + helps family understand what is happening. Patients are generally NOT distressed by the physiologic changes others perceive as distressing (mottling, Cheyne-Stokes, agonal respirations, terminal secretions). HEARING is typically the last sense to fade — encourage family to continue meaningful presence + speech. NO URGENCY at moment of death — the body can remain undisturbed for hours + family can take their time. RN pronouncement varies by state (in CA, RNs can pronounce expected hospice deaths per hospice protocol). DOCUMENTATION: time of death + family present + circumstances + body care wishes + funeral arrangements + bereavement contact. Pause to honor + acknowledge

labs.v: AT MOMENT OF DEATH: pause + honor + presence; if RN pronounces (per state scope + hospice protocol): assess for absence of respirations + pulse + heart sounds + corneal reflexes + pupillary response (typically 5-10 min of observation); document time of death + family present + circumstances; if RN does not pronounce, call attending or hospice medical director per protocol; OFFER family TIME — no rush to move body, perform procedures; ask family about RITUALS they wish (prayers, washing, dressing, music, silence); BODY CARE per family wishes + cultural/spiritual preferences: bathing, hair brushing, dressing (in clothing of family's choice if available), positioning, mouth + eye care (gently close eyes if family wishes), removing tubes/lines (if not needed for post-mortem exam), placing pillow under head, hands at sides or folded, sheets to chest; OFFER family TIME to be present with body — can be hours; FUNERAL HOME contact per family wishes (have family's preferred funeral home contact ready or hospice can coordinate); POSSESSIONS gathered + given to family; SYMPATHY card from team; CONTACT bereavement coordinator for follow-up; ORGAN/TISSUE DONATION if applicable (most expected hospice deaths not eligible due to malignancy + multi-system involvement; rare cases may donate corneas — check institutional policy + family wishes); AUTOPSY rarely needed for expected hospice death (medical cause of death known); BEREAVEMENT services begin: 13-month support per MEDICARE HOSPICE BENEFIT (calls, visits, support groups, written materials, anniversary outreach); SOCIAL WORK coordinates; CHAPLAIN follow-up; PCP notification; INSURANCE/EMPLOYER paperwork support; FAMILY follow-up at 2 weeks + 1 month + 3 month + 6 month + 9 month + 12 month + 13 month with bereavement coordinator; HIGH-RISK BEREAVEMENT (anticipatory loss, traumatic loss, family system stress, mental health history) may benefit from more intensive support or referral to grief therapy/groups; TEAM debriefing for clinical team — caring for dying patients takes emotional toll; honor + grief + meaning-making + self-care for staff
labs.r: POST-DEATH care is the final act of patient + family-centered care. PAUSE + HONOR + PRESENCE before tasks. Family TIME is sacred — no rush. RITUALS per family wishes. RN pronouncement scope varies by state. DOCUMENTATION is legal record + supports bereavement + closure. BEREAVEMENT services per Medicare hospice benefit = 13 months. Standard intervals + adapted to family needs. High-risk bereavement may need additional support. TEAM care: clinicians caring for dying patients need ongoing support + debriefing + meaning-making + self-care to prevent burnout + sustain compassionate care over time

labs.v: No uncontrolled symptoms after admission interventions (pain dropping from 7/10 to 2-3/10 on PCA + ketamine + adjuvants; nausea controlled with multi-mechanism antiemetics; delirium calmer with haloperidol + environmental modifications; secretions reduced with glycopyrrolate + repositioning); no SEIZURES, no overt SUFFERING (patient calm + minimally responsive but no grimacing or distress signs); FAMILY engaged + supported + aligned with goals of care + grateful for hospice care; advance directives + POLST in chart + clear; faith community supportive; financial + housing stable; no SUSPECTED SUICIDAL ideation (terminally ill patients distinguished from suicidal; existential distress addressed via chaplain + therapy when applicable); no estranged family in conflict (would require ethics + social work); no contested goals of care (family + patient aligned); no inadequate insurance (Medicare hospice benefit active); no UNADDRESSED grief (anticipatory grief acknowledged + supported)
labs.r: Reassuring + protective factors. Symptom management successful within first hours of inpatient admission. Family aligned + supported. Advance directives clear. Faith community + financial stability + insurance coverage all supportive. This sets up a 'good death' — relief of suffering, presence of loved ones, meaning-making, dignity, peace

nursesNote: 1400 — 62 y/o male S. Whitfield admitted to inpatient hospice unit (Bed 4 — private room with family bed + recliner + quiet environment + bedside courtyard window) for refractory pain + nausea + emerging terminal delirium + terminal secretions + caregiver fatigue from 3 months of home hospice. DIAGNOSIS: metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PDAC) head of pancreas with multiple liver mets + peritoneal carcinomatosis; diagnosed 8 months ago; progressed through FOLFIRINOX (4 cycles); declined 2nd-line gem-nab-pac after discussion of toxicity vs minimal expected benefit + preference for quality time; enrolled in home hospice 3 months ago; biliary stent placed 5 months ago for obstruction; cachexia (weight loss 30 kg / 38% body weight over 8 months — current 48 kg / BMI 16); jaundice + ascites + bilateral ankle edema. HOSPICE ELIGIBILITY: Medicare Hospice Benefit certified ≤6 months life expectancy at enrollment + recertifications; PROGNOSIS at this admission: hours to days based on functional decline + vital trajectory + symptom burden + multi-organ involvement + Cheyne-Stokes + mottling. GOALS OF CARE clarified through serial family meetings: COMFORT-FOCUSED CARE (Allow Natural Death — AND), no resuscitation/intubation/ICU transfer, no artificial nutrition/hydration unless explicitly for symptom relief (not life-extension), no antibiotics unless for comfort; focus on PAIN + SYMPTOM RELIEF + DIGNITY + FAMILY PRESENCE + SPIRITUAL/EMOTIONAL SUPPORT; signed POLST in chart with full DNR/DNI + AND; HEALTHCARE PROXY (wife) activated; advance directives reviewed. VITALS 1400: HR 112, BP 88/54, RR 24 with emerging Cheyne-Stokes, SpO2 91% on RA (declined nasal cannula per prior wishes; comfort-focused — monitoring de-emphasized; gentle fan + repositioning instead), T 38.0°C (terminal fever — acetaminophen for comfort if appears uncomfortable + cooling measures gently); mottling beginning at knees + lower legs. PAIN MANAGEMENT (refractory pain requiring inpatient escalation): home regimen of morphine ER 60 mg PO q8h + IR breakthrough + subcutaneous morphine PCA basal 4 mg/hr + 2 mg q15 min PRN failed (pain remained 6-8/10 with frequent breakthrough + nausea + opioid metabolite accumulation concern with renal decline); INPATIENT ESCALATION: rotated to subcutaneous HYDROMORPHONE PCA (improved efficacy + less renal metabolite accumulation; conversion ~5:1 morphine:hydromorphone SQ adjusted for incomplete cross-tolerance starting at 0.5 mg/hr basal + 0.2 mg q15 min PRN); LOW-DOSE KETAMINE infusion 0.1 mg/kg/hr SQ adjuvant for refractory neuropathic pain + opioid-sparing; ACETAMINOPHEN 650 mg PR q6h round-the-clock (fever + opioid-sparing); DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg IV/SQ daily continued (bone mets pain + anorexia + nausea + fatigue + general well-being); pain dropping to 2-3/10 within 2 hr of escalation. NAUSEA MANAGEMENT (multi-mechanism): HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg SQ q6h scheduled + q4h PRN (CTZ blockade — best for opioid + metabolic nausea + adjunct for terminal delirium), METOCLOPRAMIDE 10 mg SQ q6h (prokinetic for gastroparesis component — caution if complete obstruction develops), OLANZAPINE 2.5 mg SL daily (broad-spectrum antiemetic + appetite + mood + sleep; particularly useful in end-of-life multi-mechanism nausea), DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg daily continued, LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg SL q4h PRN for anxiety component, GINGER + cool low-stimulus environment; nausea controlled to mild. TERMINAL DELIRIUM management (emerging past 48 hr — hyperactive features + hypoactive somnolence): REMOVE contributors (opioid rotation done; ensure bowel emptying — last BM 4 days ago, gave bisacodyl PR + scheduled methylnaltrexone SQ; consider Foley for urinary retention if uncomfortable; address pain — controlled); HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg SQ q6h scheduled + q2h PRN preferred first-line for terminal delirium (start low + titrate); LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg SL q4h PRN for anxiety/agitation breakthrough (use cautiously — paradoxical effect possible); environmental: quiet single room with family at bedside + familiar music (his favorite jazz playing softly) + soft lighting + minimal tethering + no overhead lights at night + gentle reorientation + spiritual rituals supported (family prays + Methodist pastor visits + communion at bedside); FAMILY EDUCATION about terminal delirium as part of dying process (not pain or suffering per se; reassurance + meaningful presence + touch + music more impactful than verbal redirection; he may seem to 'see' or 'speak with' deceased relatives — this is common end-of-life experience). TERMINAL SECRETIONS ('death rattle') emerging 4-6 hr after admission (saliva + bronchial secretions pooling as he loses cough + swallow + becomes less responsive); REPOSITIONED to side-lying with HOB elevated for drainage; gentle oral suctioning of accessible secretions only (no deep suctioning — distressing + minimally effective + causes more); GLYCOPYRROLATE 0.2 mg SQ q4h PRN initiated (preferred over atropine — does not cross BBB so less delirium risk); FAMILY EDUCATION CENTRAL — explained sounds are pooling secretions because he no longer has strength to clear them; sounds distressing but NOT distressing TO HIM because diminished consciousness; we are doing what we can but cannot eliminate completely; this is normal part of last hours; family validated emotionally + provided reassurance. DYSPNEA + RESPIRATORY support: emerging Cheyne-Stokes pattern (irregular crescendo-decrescendo with apneic pauses — common in last hours, indicates brainstem changes, NOT distressing for patient); LOW-DOSE morphine via PCA gold standard for dyspnea (mechanism: reduces ventilatory drive + sensation + anxiety + preload; no respiratory depression at appropriate doses in opioid-tolerant patient with appropriate titration); LORAZEPAM 0.5 mg SL q4h PRN for anxiety component (synergistic with opioid); GENTLE FAN at low setting blowing across face (vagal stimulation reduces dyspnea sensation — evidence-based + non-pharmacologic); positioning semi-Fowler; supplemental O2 declined per his prior wishes — comfort-focused not SpO2-targeted; BiPAP/intubation not indicated (DNI + counter to goals); ETHICAL principle of DOUBLE EFFECT: aggressive symptom management with appropriate intent (comfort) is permissible even if foreseeably but unintentionally hastens death. OTHER SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT: CONSTIPATION (no BM x 4 days) — bisacodyl PR given + methylnaltrexone SQ scheduled q48h; manual disimpaction if needed with adequate analgesia + anxiolysis; ALL OPIOIDS REQUIRE BOWEL REGIMEN; ANXIETY — lorazepam + chaplain + music + touch + family presence + communication about fears; ANOREXIA — NOT force feeding (anorexia is normal at end of life + forced feeding causes distress + does not improve outcomes); ice chips for pleasure if able + small bites of favorite foods if patient enjoys; family educated; ORAL CARE q2-4h with foam swabs + water/saline + lip balm + glycerin avoidance; DRY MOUTH — moisturizers; FATIGUE — rest + family + meaningful activities while alert (we facilitated FaceTime call with adult son who is flying in tonight); SKIN CARE — pressure injury prevention with gentle repositioning q2-4h (less if causing distress in last hours — comfort wins), pressure-redistribution surface, barrier creams; PRURITUS from cholestasis — cool environment + oatmeal baths; URINARY RETENTION — Foley placed at admission for comfort + reducing urinary frequency at end of life. CAREGIVER FATIGUE + family support: wife (60, primary caregiver x 3 months, exhausted, lost 6 kg, hasn't had meal away from bedside in weeks), adult daughter (32, FMLA x 6 weeks, secondary caregiver, anticipatory grief), adult son (35, lives across country, flying in tonight — feels guilt at distance), extended family + faith community + Methodist church + pastor + congregation supportive; INPATIENT HOSPICE provides RESPITE for family while continuing patient care; SOCIAL WORK consult activated (assessment + resources + funeral planning conversation when family ready); CHAPLAIN consult (Methodist pastor primary; hospital chaplain available); BEREAVEMENT services per MEDICARE HOSPICE BENEFIT — 13-month support for family after death (phone calls, visits, support groups, written materials, anniversary outreach); family encouraged to RESPITE during inpatient stay (eat meals, sleep elsewhere, take breaks — RN provides updates); FAMILY MEETINGS to review prognosis + symptom plan + last hours expectations + post-death plans; CULTURAL + spiritual rituals supported (family's faith tradition includes communion + prayer at bedside; pastor coordinates). LAST HOURS EDUCATION provided to family in private conversation with daughter + wife (son will receive on arrival tonight): physiologic changes to expect (CARDIOVASCULAR — tachycardia then bradycardia, hypotension, mottling, cool extremities, weak peripheral pulses; RESPIRATORY — Cheyne-Stokes, agonal respirations, terminal secretions, lengthening apneic pauses until final apnea; NEUROLOGIC — declining responsiveness, may seem to 'see' deceased relatives, HEARING typically LAST sense to fade — encouraged family to continue speaking + meaningful presence even when patient appears unresponsive; GENITOURINARY — decreased then absent urine; GI — no interest in food/drink which is NORMAL; SKIN — cool + mottled + cyanotic + jaundice deepening); REDUCED FAMILY FEAR + supports meaningful presence; what to do at moment of death — there is NO URGENCY; body can remain undisturbed for hours; family can take time to be present, say goodbye, perform rituals, call other family; RN will assess + document + pronounce per California hospice protocol (RNs can pronounce expected hospice deaths in CA per Welfare & Institutions Code + hospice protocol — does not require physician at bedside for expected hospice death); body care per family wishes + cultural/spiritual preferences; funeral home contact ready per family preference. PLAN: continue comfort-focused care with frequent reassessment + titration of medications; family at bedside encouraged + supported; respite for family encouraged; chaplain + social work engaged; bedside presence with frequent gentle reorientation + music + family voice; anticipate death within hours to days; documentation throughout; post-death care per family wishes + protocol; bereavement services initiated for 13-month support per Medicare benefit; team debriefing post-event for emotional support of staff (caring for dying patients takes toll). 1800 UPDATE: pain controlled 2/10 (observed comfort signs), nausea mild, secretions reduced with glycopyrrolate + positioning, delirium calmer with haloperidol + family presence + music + soft lighting, mottling extending up thighs, minimally responsive to voice (responds to deep touch with grimace), Cheyne-Stokes more pronounced with longer apneic pauses, family at bedside (wife, daughter, son arriving 2030), faith community present briefly with prayer + communion offered + accepted, anticipate death tonight or tomorrow; family preparing emotionally + spiritually + has funeral home contact ready (Smith Funeral Home — family's longtime relationship); will call additional family if status changes acutely; team debriefing planned post-event for emotional support of all involved staff.
providerOrders: ADMIT to inpatient hospice unit (Bed 4 — private room with family bed + recliner + quiet environment + spiritual care availability + bedside courtyard window); HOSPICE LEVEL of CARE: General Inpatient (GIP — for acute symptom management not manageable at home or in other settings)

DIAGNOSIS: metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PDAC, head of pancreas with liver + peritoneal mets); CACHEXIA; refractory pain + nausea; emerging terminal delirium + terminal secretions; estimated prognosis hours to days

GOALS OF CARE: COMFORT-FOCUSED (Allow Natural Death — AND); NO CPR/intubation/ICU/artificial nutrition/hydration unless explicitly for symptom relief; NO antibiotics unless for comfort (e.g., UTI causing discomfort); FOCUS on pain + symptom relief + dignity + family presence + spiritual/emotional support
CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI documented + signed POLST in chart; HEALTHCARE PROXY (wife) activated; advance directives reviewed
PAIN MANAGEMENT:

HYDROMORPHONE subcutaneous PCA — basal 0.5 mg/hr + 0.2 mg q15 min PRN breakthrough (rotated from morphine for improved efficacy + less renal metabolite accumulation; conversion ~5:1 morphine:hydromorphone SQ with reduction for incomplete cross-tolerance); titrate to comfort + reassess q2-4h initially then q4h
KETAMINE infusion 0.1 mg/kg/hr SQ adjuvant for refractory neuropathic pain + opioid-sparing (low-dose start; titrate cautiously avoiding sedation + dysphoria)
ACETAMINOPHEN 650 mg PR q6h round-the-clock (fever + opioid-sparing)
DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg IV/SQ daily (bone mets pain + anorexia + nausea + fatigue + general well-being)
ALL OPIOIDS require BOWEL REGIMEN (see Constipation orders)
Pain reassessment q2-4h initially then q4h with observation-based assessment when self-report not feasible (grimace, restlessness, vocalization, vital changes)
NAUSEA MANAGEMENT (multi-mechanism):
HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg SQ q6h scheduled + q2-4h PRN (CTZ blockade; best for opioid + metabolic nausea + adjunct for terminal delirium)
METOCLOPRAMIDE 10 mg SQ q6h (prokinetic for gastroparesis; HOLD if complete bowel obstruction develops or QT prolongation/EPS)
OLANZAPINE 2.5 mg SL daily (broad-spectrum antiemetic + appetite + mood + sleep)
DEXAMETHASONE 4 mg daily continued
LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL q4h PRN for anticipatory/anxiety component
AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly (sedation + EPS + delirium)
Non-pharmacologic: small frequent meals if tolerated, cool foods, ginger, low-stimulus environment
TERMINAL DELIRIUM management:
REMOVE contributors when possible: opioid rotation done, ensure bowel emptying, Foley placed for urinary retention/comfort, treat pain (controlled), gentle environment
HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg SQ q6h scheduled + q2h PRN for agitation (preferred first-line for terminal delirium; less anticholinergic; minimal sedation at low doses)
LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL q4-6h PRN for breakthrough anxiety/agitation (use cautiously — paradoxical effect possible)

Environmental: quiet single room, family at bedside, familiar music (his favorite jazz), soft lighting, no overhead lights at night, minimize line/tube tethering, gentle reorientation, spiritual rituals supported
AVOID anticholinergics (worsen delirium)

FAMILY EDUCATION: terminal delirium is part of dying process (not pain or suffering per se); meaningful presence + touch + music more impactful than verbal redirection; he may seem to 'see' deceased relatives — common end-of-life experience
TERMINAL SECRETIONS ('death rattle') management:
REPOSITION to side-lying or semi-prone with HOB elevated (drainage)
GENTLE oral suctioning of ACCESSIBLE secretions only (NO DEEP SUCTIONING — distressing + minimally effective + causes more secretions)
GLYCOPYRROLATE 0.2-0.4 mg SQ q4-6h PRN (preferred — does not cross BBB so less delirium risk)
Alternative: ATROPINE 0.4 mg SQ/SL q4-6h OR SCOPOLAMINE 1.5 mg transdermal patch q72h
Optimize EARLY — anticholinergics prevent accumulation better than dry existing
REDUCE IV fluids if applicable (worsen secretions in dying)

FAMILY EDUCATION CENTRAL: explain pooling secretions + sounds distressing but NOT to HIM (diminished consciousness); cannot eliminate completely; normal part of last hours; provide reassurance + presence
DYSPNEA + respiratory support:
LOW-DOSE MORPHINE via PCA + 2-5 mg PO/SL/SQ q4h PRN (gold standard for dyspnea at end of life — mechanism: reduces ventilatory drive + sensation + anxiety + preload)
LORAZEPAM 0.5-1 mg SL/SQ q4-6h PRN for anxiety component (synergistic with opioid)
Fan at low setting blowing across face (vagal stimulation reduces sensation — evidence-based non-pharmacologic)
Positioning semi-Fowler or upright if tolerated
Supplemental O2 ONLY if hypoxic + perceived to help patient comfort (NOT target SpO2 — comfort-focused)
NO BiPAP/CPAP/HFNC unless explicitly helping symptoms; NO intubation (DNI + counter to goals)
ETHICAL: double-effect principle — aggressive symptom management with comfort intent is permissible even if foreseeably hastens death; document intent + assessment
CONSTIPATION (no BM x 4 days at admission):
Bisacodyl 10 mg PR x 1 + senna 8.6 mg PO BID (when PO tolerated) + polyethylene glycol 17 g PO daily
METHYLNALTREXONE 8-12 mg SQ q48h scheduled (opioid-induced refractory — peripheral mu antagonist; does not reverse analgesia)
Manual disimpaction by RN if needed (gentle, with adequate analgesia + anxiolysis)
ALL OPIOIDS require BOWEL REGIMEN — frequently missed
OTHER SYMPTOM management:
ANXIETY: lorazepam + chaplain + music + touch + family presence + communication about fears

ANOREXIA: DO NOT force feeding (normal at end of life + forced feeding distressing + does not improve outcomes); ice chips for pleasure + small bites of favorite foods if patient enjoys; FAMILY EDUCATION CRITICAL
ORAL CARE q2-4h with foam swabs + water/saline + lip balm + AVOID glycerin (dries mucosa)
DRY MOUTH — sip water if able + ice chips + oral moisturizers + lip care
FATIGUE: rest + family + meaningful activities while alert (memory book, music, video calls with distant family — facilitated FaceTime with son)

SKIN CARE: pressure injury prevention with gentle repositioning q2-4h (less if causing distress in last hours — COMFORT WINS over textbook protocol), pressure-redistribution surface, barrier creams for incontinence
PRURITUS from cholestasis: cool environment, oatmeal baths, low-dose naltrexone or sertraline (sometimes effective), antihistamines limited use, topical menthol
URINARY RETENTION: Foley placed at admission for comfort + reducing urinary frequency at end of life
VITAL SIGNS + monitoring:
Comfort-focused assessment — observation-based when self-report not feasible (facial expression, body posture, vocalization, breathing pattern, color, perfusion, responsiveness)
SpO2 monitoring de-emphasized (comfort-focused not target-driven)
Cardiac monitoring NOT needed (counter to goals)
Labs NOT routinely drawn (counter to comfort-focused care unless specifically guiding symptom management)
Frequency: full assessment q4h + as needed if symptoms change
FAMILY SUPPORT:
SOCIAL WORK consult activated (assessment + resources + caregiver support + funeral planning conversation when family ready + insurance/employer paperwork support)
CHAPLAIN consult (Methodist pastor primary; hospital chaplain available; multi-faith respect)
BEREAVEMENT services per MEDICARE HOSPICE BENEFIT — 13-month support after death (calls, visits, support groups, written materials, anniversary outreach)
RESPITE for family during inpatient stay (encouraged to leave bedside, eat meals, sleep elsewhere — RN provides updates)
FAMILY MEETINGS to review prognosis + symptom plan + last hours expectations + post-death plans
CULTURAL + spiritual rituals supported (Methodist faith tradition; communion + prayer at bedside; pastor coordinates)
COMMUNICATION: honest, simple language; updates as conditions change; presence + meaningful touch + listening
LAST HOURS family education:
CARDIOVASCULAR — tachycardia then bradycardia, hypotension, MOTTLING (painless for patient), cool extremities, weak/absent peripheral pulses
RESPIRATORY — Cheyne-Stokes pattern (NOT distressing for patient), agonal respirations (reflexive NOT conscious distress), terminal secretions, lengthening apneic pauses until final apnea

NEUROLOGIC — declining responsiveness, may seem to 'see' deceased relatives (encourage family not to dismiss — meaningful), HEARING typically LAST sense to fade (encourage continued speech + meaningful presence)
GENITOURINARY — decreased then absent urine output
GI — no interest in food/drink (NORMAL — do not force)
SKIN — cool + mottled + cyanotic + jaundice deepening
AT MOMENT OF DEATH: NO URGENCY; body can remain undisturbed for hours; family can take time to be present, say goodbye, perform rituals, call other family
POST-DEATH care:
PAUSE + HONOR + PRESENCE before tasks

RN PRONOUNCEMENT per California scope + hospice protocol (CA RNs can pronounce expected hospice deaths — document time of death + family present + circumstances + body care + family wishes); call attending or hospice medical director if not RN-pronouncing
OFFER family TIME — no rush to move body or perform procedures
ASK family about RITUALS they wish (prayers, washing, dressing, music, silence)

BODY CARE per family wishes + cultural/spiritual preferences: bathing, hair brushing, dressing, positioning, gentle eye/mouth care (close eyes if family wishes), removing tubes/lines (if not needed for post-mortem exam), placing pillow under head, hands at sides or folded, sheets to chest
POSSESSIONS gathered + given to family
FUNERAL HOME contact per family preference (Smith Funeral Home — family's longtime relationship)
SYMPATHY card from team
CONTACT bereavement coordinator for 13-month follow-up
PCP notification + insurance/employer paperwork support
ORGAN/TISSUE DONATION generally not applicable (malignancy + multi-system) but check institutional + family wishes; corneas sometimes possible
AUTOPSY rarely needed for expected hospice death (cause of death known); family choice if desired
TEAM DEBRIEFING + emotional support (caring for dying patients takes toll):
Post-event debriefing for clinical team
EAP + peer support + chaplain available
Recognize moral distress + provide space for processing + meaning-making
Self-care practices encouraged + supported by leadership
Sustainability of compassionate care requires team well-being

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this inpatient hospice admission?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically + ethically significant cues requiring nursing attention at this comfort-focused admission.

opts.text: REFRACTORY pain (7/10 at admission on home subcutaneous morphine PCA + adjuvants — failed to control); inpatient escalation needed: rotated to hydromorphone PCA + low-dose ketamine adjuvant + scheduled acetaminophen + dexamethasone; pain dropping to 2-3/10 within 2 hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — refractory pain is the most common reason for inpatient hospice admission. Opioid rotation when one not effective (cross-tolerance incomplete). Subcutaneous route when PO failing. Multimodal (adjuvants for neuropathic + bone + inflammation). KETAMINE low-dose adjuvant for refractory. ALL OPIOIDS need BOWEL REGIMEN.

opts.text: MULTI-MECHANISM nausea (opioid-induced CTZ + gastroparesis + metabolic + anxiety): haloperidol + metoclopramide + olanzapine + dexamethasone + lorazepam; mechanism-based combination therapy; AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — mechanism-based nausea management. HALOPERIDOL gold standard for opioid + metabolic CTZ nausea. METOCLOPRAMIDE for gastroparesis. OLANZAPINE broad-spectrum. DEXAMETHASONE for ICP + multi-purpose. LORAZEPAM for anticipatory. AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly (sedation + EPS + delirium).

opts.text: TERMINAL DELIRIUM emerging past 48 hr (hyperactive + hypoactive features): remove contributors (opioid rotation, bowel, urinary, pain control, environment); HALOPERIDOL 0.5-1 mg SQ q6h + q2h PRN preferred first-line; LORAZEPAM PRN for breakthrough; quiet familiar environment + family + music + soft lighting; FAMILY EDUCATION central
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — terminal delirium ~80% of patients in last days. Multifactorial. HALOPERIDOL gold standard. Address reversible contributors. AVOID anticholinergics (worsen). Environmental measures. FAMILY EDUCATION (this is part of dying, not pain/suffering; meaningful presence > rational redirection).

opts.text: TERMINAL SECRETIONS ('death rattle') emerging 4-6 hr after admission: repositioning + gentle oral suctioning of accessible only (NO deep — distressing + causes more) + GLYCOPYRROLATE SQ (preferred over atropine — does not cross BBB so less delirium); FAMILY EDUCATION CENTRAL (sounds distressing but NOT to HIM — diminished consciousness)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — terminal secretions in 35-90% of dying patients. Family distress often greater than patient distress. Anticholinergics work best EARLY before significant accumulation. FAMILY EDUCATION is the most important nursing intervention (explain what is happening + why patient is not aware + that we cannot eliminate completely).

opts.text: DYSPNEA + Cheyne-Stokes breathing pattern (common in last hours — indicates brainstem changes; NOT distressing for patient): low-dose MORPHINE via PCA (gold standard for dyspnea — reduces ventilatory drive + sensation + anxiety + preload), LORAZEPAM, fan, positioning; SpO2 monitoring de-emphasized (comfort-focused not target-driven)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — dyspnea management at end of life. LOW-DOSE OPIOID gold standard. Cheyne-Stokes is common + does not require intervention beyond family education. Non-pharmacologic adjuncts evidence-based (fan = vagal stimulation reduces sensation). DOUBLE-EFFECT ethics permits aggressive symptom management with comfort intent.

opts.text: CAREGIVER FATIGUE: wife exhausted (3 mo home hospice + lost 6 kg herself + sleep-deprived), daughter on FMLA, son flying in tonight from distance + guilt; INPATIENT HOSPICE provides RESPITE while continuing patient care; social work + chaplain + bereavement services activated
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — caregiver burden is recognized health issue with increased caregiver morbidity + mortality. Inpatient hospice provides respite (5 days/month per Medicare benefit). Family-centered care extends through patient's life + bereavement. 13-MONTH bereavement support per Medicare hospice regs.

opts.text: LAST HOURS family education provided: physiologic changes to expect (mottling + cool extremities + Cheyne-Stokes + agonal respirations + terminal secretions + declining responsiveness + HEARING LAST SENSE TO FADE); reduces fear + supports meaningful presence; what to do at moment of death (NO URGENCY)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — one of the most important nursing interventions in EOL care. Educating family about what to expect reduces fear + supports meaningful presence. Hearing typically last sense to fade — encourage continued speech + presence even when patient appears unresponsive. NO URGENCY at moment of death — body can remain undisturbed for hours.

opts.text: GOALS OF CARE clarified through serial family meetings: COMFORT-FOCUSED (Allow Natural Death); no CPR/intubation/ICU/artificial nutrition or hydration; focus on pain + symptom relief + dignity + family presence + spiritual/emotional support; signed POLST + DNR/DNI in chart; healthcare proxy (wife) activated
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — goals of care guide all care decisions + must be reviewed at transitions. POLST translates wishes into medical orders that follow patient across settings. Healthcare proxy activated when patient decisional capacity fails. Goals of care conversations are ongoing + evolve.

opts.text: POST-DEATH care planning: RN pronouncement per California scope + hospice protocol; pause + honor + presence; family TIME; rituals per family wishes; body care; funeral home (Smith Funeral Home — family's longtime); bereavement services begin (13-month per Medicare hospice benefit); team debriefing for staff emotional support
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — post-death care + bereavement + team care. Final act of patient + family-centered care. Pause + honor + presence before tasks. RN pronouncement varies by state (CA permits for expected hospice deaths). Bereavement 13-month support per Medicare. Team care (debriefing + EAP + peer support) sustains compassionate practice.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed. No medication allergies to consider in symptom management regimen selection.

opts.text: SpO2 91% on room air — need to escalate oxygen therapy to high-flow nasal cannula immediately + escalate to ICU for monitoring
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach in comfort-focused hospice care. SpO2 monitoring is DE-EMPHASIZED (comfort-focused not target-driven); ICU transfer is counter to goals + POLST + AND status. Supplemental O2 only if hypoxic + perceived to help patient comfort (NOT target SpO2). HFNC/BiPAP/intubation not indicated unless explicitly helping symptoms (rare in terminal decline). Comfort wins over textbook protocol.

opts.text: Patient has no interest in food — must initiate enteral feeding via NG tube or PEG placement to prevent malnutrition + improve outcomes
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach at end of life. ANOREXIA is normal at end of life + forced feeding causes distress + does not improve outcomes (the body is not 'starving' in the way an otherwise healthy person would be). FAMILY EDUCATION is critical (loss of interest in food + drink is part of dying). Small bites of favorite foods if patient enjoys + tolerates; ice chips for pleasure; oral care for comfort. NG/PEG placement contradicts goals of care + causes harm without benefit.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What ELNEC principles + symptom management apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying ELNEC + symptom management principles.

opts.text: PAIN MANAGEMENT principles: WHO analgesic ladder + individualized; regular round-the-clock + as-needed breakthrough; mechanism-based selection (opioids + adjuvants for nociceptive; gabapentin/pregabalin/duloxetine/TCAs for neuropathic; corticosteroids for bone/inflammation; bisphosphonates/radiation for bone mets); OPIOID ROTATION when one not effective (cross-tolerance incomplete — switch + reduce 25-50% then titrate); SUBCUTANEOUS route when oral not feasible; PCA empowers; KETAMINE low-dose adjuvant for refractory; ALL OPIOIDS need BOWEL REGIMEN; UNDERTREATING pain is common error
opts.rat: CORRECT — comprehensive pain management framework. Most cancer pain is controllable with appropriate regimen. Mechanism-based + multimodal approach. Subcutaneous route reliable when PO failing. Aggressive within comfort goals. Ethical principle of double-effect permits aggressive symptom management with comfort intent.

opts.text: NAUSEA MANAGEMENT — MECHANISM-BASED (Brooks/Twycross): VESTIBULAR (motion-related) → meclizine/cyclizine/scopolamine; MECHANICAL/gastric stasis → metoclopramide (prokinetic; avoid if complete obstruction); CHEMORECEPTOR TRIGGER ZONE (opioid + metabolic) → haloperidol (gold standard) OR olanzapine (broad-spectrum); INCREASED ICP → dexamethasone + 5HT3 antagonist; ANTICIPATORY/anxiety → lorazepam; MULTI-MECHANISM → combination therapy; AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly (sedation/EPS/delirium)
opts.rat: CORRECT — mechanism-based nausea framework. Different mechanisms require different agents. Combination therapy for multi-mechanism. Avoid certain agents in elderly. Reassess + titrate frequently. Address contributing factors (constipation, dehydration, anxiety, food preferences). Olanzapine + dexamethasone + haloperidol often effective combination.

opts.text: TERMINAL DELIRIUM ~80% of patients in last days of life; multifactorial; MANAGEMENT: remove contributors when feasible (opioid rotation, urinary retention, fecal impaction, hypercalcemia, oversedation); HALOPERIDOL gold standard first-line (less anticholinergic + minimal sedation at low doses); LORAZEPAM PRN for breakthrough (cautiously — paradoxical effect possible); environmental measures (quiet familiar single room + family + music + soft lighting + minimal tethering); AVOID anticholinergics (worsen); FAMILY EDUCATION (part of dying, not pain/suffering; meaningful presence > verbal redirection)
opts.rat: CORRECT — terminal delirium management framework. Family often more distressed than patient (who is not in pain per se). Explain what is happening + meaningful presence + reassurance. Avoid restraints (not appropriate in EOL). Spiritual + cultural rituals supported. Reversible causes addressed (urinary retention, fecal impaction, oversedation).

opts.text: TERMINAL SECRETIONS in 35-90% of dying patients; MANAGEMENT pearls: (1) FAMILY distress often greater than patient distress, (2) anticholinergics work best EARLY before significant accumulation, (3) DEEP SUCTIONING counterproductive (stimulates more + distresses patient), (4) REPOSITIONING + gentle oral care + FAMILY EDUCATION central, (5) GLYCOPYRROLATE preferred over atropine (less CNS delirium risk), (6) REDUCE IV fluids if contributing, (7) FAMILY EDUCATION about what is happening + why we cannot eliminate completely + that patient is NOT AWARE = MOST IMPORTANT nursing intervention
opts.rat: CORRECT — terminal secretions framework. Family education is central — addresses the distress of those who CAN perceive the situation. Patient is generally not distressed (diminished consciousness). Repositioning + early anticholinergics + avoiding deep suctioning + family support.

opts.text: DYSPNEA at end of life: LOW-DOSE MORPHINE gold standard (reduces ventilatory drive + sensation + anxiety + preload — multi-modal mechanism); LORAZEPAM for anxiety component; FAN at low setting blowing across face (vagal stimulation reduces sensation — evidence-based); positioning; supplemental O2 only if hypoxic + perceived to help comfort (NOT target SpO2); BiPAP/intubation not indicated in comfort-focused care; DOUBLE-EFFECT ethics permits aggressive symptom management with comfort intent
opts.rat: CORRECT — dyspnea management framework. Low-dose opioid evidence-based + gold standard. Opioid 'fear' largely unfounded at appropriate doses in opioid-tolerant patients with appropriate titration. Non-pharmacologic adjuncts (fan, positioning, environment, presence) evidence-based + complementary. Comfort-focused care does not target SpO2.

opts.text: GOALS OF CARE clarified through serial conversations + POLST: COMFORT-FOCUSED (Allow Natural Death); HEALTHCARE PROXY activated when patient decisional capacity fails; advance directives reviewed at every transition; HOSPICE ELIGIBILITY (Medicare Hospice Benefit) = life expectancy ≤6 mo if disease runs natural course; services include nursing + aide + social work + chaplain + bereavement + medical director + medications + equipment + 24/7 access + respite + inpatient when needed
opts.rat: CORRECT — goals of care + hospice eligibility framework. POLST translates wishes into medical orders that follow across settings. Healthcare proxy activated when patient capacity fails. Conversations ongoing + evolve. Medicare Hospice Benefit comprehensive services for both patient + family.

opts.text: CAREGIVER BURDEN recognized health issue with increased caregiver morbidity + mortality; INPATIENT HOSPICE provides RESPITE (5 days/month per Medicare benefit); FAMILY-CENTERED care extends throughout patient's life + into bereavement; 13-MONTH BEREAVEMENT support per Medicare hospice regs (year of firsts + anniversary); social work + chaplain + spiritual/cultural rituals + community resources
opts.rat: CORRECT — caregiver + family support framework. Family-centered care is a core ELNEC competency. Respite care evidence-based for caregiver health. Bereavement support reduces complicated grief + supports adaptation. Faith community + cultural rituals respected + integrated.

opts.text: LAST HOURS physiologic changes to expect + educate family: cardiovascular (tachycardia → bradycardia, hypotension, mottling — painless for patient, cool extremities), respiratory (Cheyne-Stokes — common, brainstem changes, NOT distressing for patient; agonal respirations — reflexive NOT conscious distress; terminal secretions; lengthening apneic pauses until final apnea), neurologic (declining responsiveness; may seem to 'see' deceased relatives — meaningful; HEARING typically LAST sense to fade — encourage continued speech + presence), GU (decreased then absent UOP), GI (no interest in food/drink — NORMAL), skin (cool + mottled + cyanotic + jaundice deepens); NO URGENCY at moment of death — body can remain undisturbed for hours
opts.rat: CORRECT — last hours education framework. Educating family is one of the most important EOL interventions. Reduces fear + supports meaningful presence. Hearing typically last sense to fade. Patient generally NOT distressed by changes others perceive as distressing. No urgency at moment of death — family can take their time.

opts.text: Insist that the patient eat 3 full meals daily + drink 2 liters of fluid + ambulate twice daily — standard hospital nursing standards apply to all patients regardless of diagnosis or goals of care
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Comfort-focused hospice care reframes standard hospital protocols. ANOREXIA + decreased intake are NORMAL at end of life — forced feeding/hydration causes distress + does not improve outcomes. Family education critical. Ambulation may not be safe or appropriate as patient declines. COMFORT WINS over textbook protocol. The 'standard of care' for dying patients is symptom management + dignity + presence — NOT generic hospital nursing protocols.

opts.text: Avoid all opioids in dying patients because of risk of respiratory depression and accidental hastening of death — use only acetaminophen + non-pharmacologic approaches
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + harmful. Opioid 'fear' is largely unfounded at appropriate doses in opioid-tolerant patients with appropriate titration. UNDERTREATING PAIN + DYSPNEA causes profound suffering. Low-dose morphine is the GOLD STANDARD for dyspnea at end of life. DOUBLE-EFFECT ethics: aggressive symptom management with comfort intent is permissible even if foreseeably but unintentionally hastens death. Acetaminophen + non-pharmacologic alone is grossly inadequate for refractory cancer pain + dyspnea.

opts.text: Apply soft wrist restraints to prevent the patient from pulling at lines and tubes during terminal delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restraints are NOT appropriate in EOL care — increase agitation + cause distress + harm dignity. Address terminal delirium with environmental measures (quiet familiar room, family at bedside, music, soft lighting, minimal tethering, gentle reorientation) + HALOPERIDOL + LORAZEPAM PRN. Remove unnecessary lines/tubes (the Foley placed for comfort is acceptable; IVs for comfort medications acceptable; unnecessary tethering removed). Family presence and meaningful touch are more effective than restraint. Restraints violate dignity + worsen delirium.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at inpatient hospice admission
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST) at this comfort-focused admission.

opts.text: Refractory Pain + Multi-Mechanism Nausea r/t metastatic pancreatic cancer + opioid metabolites accumulating in renal decline + gastroparesis + metabolic; opioid rotation to hydromorphone PCA + ketamine adjuvant + mechanism-based antiemetics (haloperidol + metoclopramide + olanzapine + dexamethasone + lorazepam)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Symptom control is the most immediate need at inpatient hospice admission + the most common reason for transfer from home. Refractory pain + nausea cause profound suffering. Aggressive within comfort goals. Pain dropped to 2-3/10 within 2 hr; nausea controlled to mild.

opts.text: Terminal Delirium + Terminal Secretions r/t end-stage disease + multifactorial contributors; haloperidol scheduled + PRN; environmental measures (quiet familiar room + family + music + soft lighting + minimal tethering); glycopyrrolate for secretions; FAMILY EDUCATION central for both
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Terminal delirium + secretions in last hours/days are common + distressing for family. Manage symptoms + educate family extensively (this is part of dying, not pain/suffering for patient). Family presence + reassurance more impactful than verbal redirection.

opts.text: Dyspnea + Cheyne-Stokes r/t terminal decline; low-dose morphine via PCA gold standard + lorazepam for anxiety + fan + positioning; SpO2 monitoring de-emphasized; double-effect ethics permits aggressive comfort management
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Dyspnea at end of life is distressing for patient + family. Low-dose opioid is gold standard. Cheyne-Stokes is common in last hours (brainstem changes; NOT distressing for patient). Non-pharmacologic adjuncts evidence-based. Comfort-focused not SpO2-targeted.

opts.text: Family Support + Caregiver Fatigue + Last Hours Education r/t 3 months home hospice exhaustion + anticipatory grief + need for prognostic clarity + cultural/spiritual rituals + post-death planning; social work + chaplain + bereavement services activated; respite during inpatient stay; family meetings + last hours education + rituals supported
opts.rat: #4 — Significant + ongoing. Family-centered care is a core ELNEC competency. Caregiver burden = recognized health issue. Inpatient hospice provides respite. 13-month bereavement support per Medicare hospice benefit. Family meetings + last hours education + spiritual/cultural rituals + bereavement planning.

opts.text: Goals of Care Maintenance + Post-Death Planning r/t comfort-focused care (AND status + POLST + DNR/DNI); RN scope at pronouncement varies by state (CA permits for expected hospice deaths); body care per family wishes + cultural/spiritual preferences; funeral home contact ready; bereavement services begin
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + at moment of death. Goals of care guide all decisions. Advance directives reviewed at every transition. RN pronouncement scope per state. Pause + honor + family time + rituals + body care + funeral coordination + bereavement initiation. The final act of patient + family-centered care.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this comfort-focused admission.

opts.text: Hydromorphone subcutaneous PCA (rotated from morphine for improved efficacy + less renal metabolite accumulation) + ketamine 0.1 mg/kg/hr SQ low-dose adjuvant + acetaminophen 650 mg PR q6h + dexamethasone 4 mg daily; titrate to comfort; bowel regimen + reassess q2-4h
opts.rat: Refractory cancer pain management. Opioid rotation when one not effective (cross-tolerance incomplete). Subcutaneous PCA when PO failing. Ketamine adjuvant for refractory neuropathic. Multimodal. ALL opioids require bowel regimen. Frequent reassessment + titration.

opts.text: Multi-mechanism antiemetic regimen: haloperidol 0.5-1 mg SQ q6h scheduled + q2-4h PRN (CTZ + opioid + delirium adjunct); metoclopramide 10 mg SQ q6h (prokinetic for gastroparesis); olanzapine 2.5 mg SL daily (broad-spectrum); dexamethasone continued; lorazepam PRN for anxiety; AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly
opts.rat: Mechanism-based nausea management for multi-mechanism etiology. Combination therapy. AVOID promethazine + prochlorperazine in elderly (sedation + EPS + delirium). Reassess + titrate frequently. Address contributing factors (constipation, dehydration, anxiety).

opts.text: Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg SQ q6h scheduled + q2h PRN for terminal delirium (preferred first-line — less anticholinergic + minimal sedation); LORAZEPAM PRN for breakthrough; environmental measures (quiet familiar single room + family + music + soft lighting + minimal tethering); AVOID anticholinergics; FAMILY EDUCATION central
opts.rat: Terminal delirium management. Haloperidol gold standard. Address reversible contributors. Environmental measures + family education. Avoid anticholinergics (worsen delirium). Family education central (part of dying, not pain/suffering; meaningful presence > verbal redirection).

opts.text: Glycopyrrolate 0.2-0.4 mg SQ q4-6h PRN for terminal secretions (preferred over atropine — does not cross BBB so less delirium risk); repositioning to side-lying with HOB elevated; gentle oral suctioning of accessible only; reduce IV fluids if contributing; FAMILY EDUCATION central
opts.rat: Terminal secretions management. Anticholinergics work best EARLY before significant accumulation. Glycopyrrolate over atropine for less CNS effects + delirium risk. No deep suctioning. Family education most important nursing intervention (explain what is happening + patient is not aware + cannot eliminate completely).

opts.text: Low-dose morphine via PCA + 2-5 mg PO/SL/SQ q4h PRN for dyspnea (gold standard at end of life — reduces ventilatory drive + sensation + anxiety + preload); LORAZEPAM for anxiety; fan at low setting blowing across face; positioning; SpO2 monitoring de-emphasized
opts.rat: Dyspnea management at end of life. Low-dose opioid gold standard (mechanism multi-modal). Non-pharmacologic adjuncts evidence-based (fan = vagal stimulation reduces sensation). Comfort-focused not SpO2-targeted. Double-effect ethics permits aggressive symptom management with comfort intent.

opts.text: Initiate enteral feeding via NG tube or PEG placement to prevent malnutrition + improve outcomes; force patient to eat 3 meals + drink 2L fluid daily
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG at end of life. ANOREXIA + decreased intake are NORMAL at end of life — forced feeding causes distress + does not improve outcomes. Family education critical (body is not 'starving' in the way an otherwise healthy person would be). Small bites of favorite foods if patient enjoys + tolerates; ice chips for pleasure; oral care for comfort. NG/PEG contradicts goals of care + causes harm without benefit.

opts.text: Escalate oxygen to high-flow nasal cannula + transfer to ICU for monitoring + intubation prep given SpO2 91% on room air + Cheyne-Stokes breathing pattern
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG in comfort-focused hospice care. SpO2 monitoring DE-EMPHASIZED (comfort-focused not target-driven); ICU transfer counter to goals + POLST + AND status; HFNC/BiPAP/intubation not indicated unless explicitly helping symptoms (rare in terminal decline). Cheyne-Stokes is common in last hours (brainstem changes; NOT distressing for patient). Supplemental O2 only if hypoxic + perceived to help comfort.

opts.text: Apply soft wrist restraints to prevent patient from pulling at lines/tubes during terminal delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restraints not appropriate in EOL care — increase agitation + harm dignity. Address delirium with environmental measures + haloperidol + lorazepam. Remove unnecessary lines/tubes. Family presence + meaningful touch more effective. Restraints violate dignity + worsen delirium.

opts.text: ALL opioids require BOWEL REGIMEN: bisacodyl PR x 1 + senna + polyethylene glycol (when PO tolerated) + methylnaltrexone SQ q48h scheduled (opioid-induced refractory — peripheral mu antagonist; does not reverse analgesia); manual disimpaction by RN with adequate analgesia + anxiolysis if needed
opts.rat: All opioids cause constipation; bowel regimen prevents fecal impaction + bowel obstruction + worsening symptoms. Methylnaltrexone for opioid-induced refractory (peripheral mu antagonist; does NOT cross BBB so does not reverse central analgesia). Manual disimpaction if needed.

opts.text: FAMILY SUPPORT: social work + chaplain + bereavement services activated; respite for family during inpatient stay; family meetings + last hours education + cultural/spiritual rituals supported; faith community engaged; 13-month bereavement per Medicare hospice benefit
opts.rat: Family-centered care is core ELNEC competency. Caregiver burden + anticipatory grief + need for prognostic clarity + ritual support all addressed. Inpatient hospice provides respite. 13-month bereavement support per Medicare hospice benefit (year of firsts + anniversary).

opts.text: LAST HOURS family education: cardiovascular (mottling, cool extremities), respiratory (Cheyne-Stokes, agonal respirations, terminal secretions), neurologic (declining responsiveness, HEARING last sense to fade — continue speech + meaningful presence), GU (decreased UOP), GI (no interest in food/drink NORMAL), skin (cool + mottled + cyanotic); NO URGENCY at moment of death
opts.rat: Educating family about what to expect reduces fear + supports meaningful presence. One of the most important EOL nursing interventions. Hearing typically last sense to fade — encourage continued speech + presence even when patient appears unresponsive. NO urgency at moment of death — family can take their time.
